overview.name: T-shirt
overview.description: The T-shirt is a ubiquitous casual wear garment in India, forming a significant part of the country's vast textile and apparel industry. India is a major global producer and exporter of T-shirts, leveraging its abundant cotton resources and skilled workforce. The domestic market for T-shirts is driven by a large youth population and increasing fashion consciousness, with production hubs like Tiruppur being globally renowned for knitwear manufacturing.
overview.origin_in_india: The heartland of T-shirt manufacturing in India is primarily the knitwear cluster of Tiruppur in Tamil Nadu. Other significant regions include Ludhiana in Punjab for hosiery and knitwear, and garment manufacturing hubs in Bengaluru (Karnataka) and Delhi-NCR.
key_organisations: Ministry of Textiles, Government of India
overview.historical_note: The history of textiles in India dates back over 5000 years, with evidence of cotton cultivation and textile production found in the Indus Valley Civilization around 3000 BCE. India was a major global textile leader, known for its fine cottons and dyed fabrics, long before modern T-shirt manufacturing.

raw_materials.name: Cotton Fibre
raw_materials.source_region: Maharashtra (Yavatmal, Akola, Amravati), Gujarat (Rajkot, Surendranagar, Bhavnagar, Amreli), Telangana (Warangal, Adilabad, Nizamabad), Karnataka (Raichur, Bellary, Haveri), Madhya Pradesh (Khargone, Khandwa, Barwani), Punjab (Bathinda, Muktsar, Fazilka), Haryana (Hisar, Sirsa, Fatehabad), Rajasthan (Ganganagar, Hanumangarh, Churu), Andhra Pradesh (Kurnool, Guntur, Anantapur), Tamil Nadu, Odisha (Koraput, Kalahandi, Rayagada).
raw_materials.cost_per_unit: Rs 6,000-8,000 per quintal (100 kg) for raw cotton (kapas) [approx. 2024-25]
raw_materials.notes: India is the largest producer of cotton globally, accounting for 23% of total global cotton production. Different varieties like MCU 5, LRA 5166, and JK Cotton Hybrid are grown.

raw_materials.name: Polyester Fibre/Yarn
raw_materials.cost_per_unit: Rs 165-170 per kg for polyester fibre (as of April 2026).
raw_materials.notes: Used for blended T-shirts, offering durability and wrinkle resistance. Prices for synthetic materials have seen increases.

supply_chain.location: Maharashtra, Gujarat, Telangana, Karnataka, Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana, Madhya Pradesh, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Odisha.
supply_chain.description: Farmers cultivate cotton as a Kharif crop, typically sowing from March-May in northern India and June-July with monsoon onset in central and southern states. Manual picking is common, involving laborers harvesting cotton bolls.
supply_chain.cost_added_inr: Rs 2,500-4,000 per quintal (for seeds, fertilizers, pesticides, labor, irrigation)

supply_chain.location: Major cotton-producing districts in Maharashtra, Gujarat, Telangana, Madhya Pradesh.
supply_chain.description: Raw cotton (kapas) is transported from farms to ginning mills where lint (fibre) is separated from seeds. The lint is then pressed into bales of 170 kg each.
supply_chain.cost_added_inr: Rs 300-500 per quintal of kapas (ginning charges, transport)

supply_chain.location: Tamil Nadu (Tiruppur, Coimbatore), Gujarat, Maharashtra, Punjab (Ludhiana).
supply_chain.description: Cotton bales are opened, cleaned, carded (fibres separated), combed (for finer yarn), drawn (fibres aligned), and then spun into yarn using ring frames or open-end spinning machines. Common yarn counts for T-shirts are 30s and 40s combed cotton.
supply_chain.cost_added_inr: Rs 50-80 per kg of yarn (processing, labor, energy). Cotton yarn prices have risen from Rs 260 per kg to around Rs 292 per kg as of April 2026.

supply_chain.description: Yarn is fed into circular knitting machines to produce tubular fabric. Single jersey is the most common knit structure for T-shirts. A typical circular knitting machine produces 200-400 kg of fabric per day.
supply_chain.cost_added_inr: Rs 20-40 per kg of grey fabric (knitting charges, labor, machine maintenance)

supply_chain.description: The grey knitted fabric undergoes pre-treatment (scouring, bleaching), dyeing to achieve desired colors, and various finishing processes like compacting, bio-washing, and calendering to enhance feel, reduce shrinkage, and improve fabric stability.
supply_chain.cost_added_inr: Rs 40-70 per kg of dyed and finished fabric (dyes, chemicals, water, energy, labor)

supply_chain.description: The finished fabric is inspected, patterns are made using CAD software, and fabric layers are precisely cut. Cut pieces are then stitched together by sewing machine operators. A basic T-shirt involves 6-8 stitching operations, while premium ones can have 10-22. This stage also includes printing or embroidery.
key_equipment: Industrial sewing machines (overlock, flatlock, single needle)
supply_chain.cost_added_inr: Rs 50-150 per T-shirt (cutting, stitching, embellishment, labor, overheads)

supply_chain.location: Major cities across India (Mumbai, Delhi, Bengaluru, Chennai, Kolkata).
supply_chain.description: Finished and packaged T-shirts are transported from manufacturing units to warehouses, distributors, and wholesalers across the country. This involves inventory management, order fulfillment, and transportation.
supply_chain.cost_added_inr: Rs 10-30 per T-shirt (transportation, warehousing, sales commission)

supply_chain.description: T-shirts are displayed and sold to end-consumers through various channels, including large format stores, brand outlets, multi-brand retailers, local shops, and e-commerce platforms.
supply_chain.cost_added_inr: Rs 50-300+ per T-shirt (store rent, staff salaries, marketing, profit margin)

major_clusters: Tiruppur, Tamil Nadu (Knitwear Capital of India)
trade_routes: Road networks connecting production clusters to major ports (Mumbai, Chennai, Mundra, Kolkata)
Air cargo for high-value or urgent international shipments
Sea routes for international exports to USA, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, UK, UAE, Germany, China, Vietnam, Indonesia.

economics.farm_gate_price_inr: Rs 6,000-8,000 per quintal (100 kg) for raw cotton (kapas) [approx. 2024-25]
economics.retail_price_inr: Rs 199 - 5,900+ per T-shirt (depending on brand, material, and design)
economics.gst_notes: Effective September 22, 2025, T-shirts and other knitted or crocheted garments are subject to 5% GST if the sale value is up to Rs 2,500 per piece, and 18% GST if the sale value is above Rs 2,500 per piece. Raw cotton is generally subject to 5% GST on a reverse charge basis.
economics.market_size_india: The Indian textile and apparel market is substantial, with the apparel segment being a major contributor. Specific market size for T-shirts is part of the broader apparel market, which is projected to grow significantly.

sustainability.carbon_footprint: The textile industry, including T-shirt manufacturing, has a significant carbon footprint due to energy-intensive processes in spinning, weaving, dyeing, and transportation. Cotton cultivation also contributes through fertilizer and pesticide use.
sustainability.water_usage: High water usage is a major concern, particularly in cotton cultivation (often rainfed, but irrigated areas consume significant water) and wet processing stages like dyeing and finishing. Water pollution from dye effluents is also a critical issue.
key_concerns: Water pollution from dyeing and finishing units (discharge of untreated effluents)
High water consumption in cotton farming and processing
Pesticide and insecticide use in conventional cotton cultivation
Textile waste from cutting and post-consumer garments
Labor conditions and wages in garment factories
certifications_schemes: Organic Cotton Certification (e.g., Global Organic Textile Standard - GOTS)
Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) initiatives in dyeing clusters

key_schemes: Production Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme for Textiles: Aims to boost domestic manufacturing and exports of high-value MMF garments, fabrics, and technical textiles with an outlay of Rs 10,683 crore.
SAMARTH (Scheme for Capacity Building in Textile Sector): Focuses on skill development and training for workers across the textile value chain, excluding spinning and weaving, to address skill gaps.

PM Mega Integrated Textile Region and Apparel (PM MITRA) Parks Scheme: Aims to develop world-class integrated textile parks with plug-and-play facilities to reduce logistics costs and improve competitiveness. Seven parks are being established with an outlay of Rs 4,445 crore.

Amended Technology Upgradation Fund Scheme (ATUFS): Provides credit-linked capital investment subsidy to textile units for technology upgradation and modernization, promoting investment and employment.
Scheme for Integrated Textile Parks (SITP): Supports the creation of world-class infrastructure facilities for textile units.
National Technical Textiles Mission (NTTM): Promotes research, innovation, and usage of technical textiles.
Rebate of State and Central Taxes and Levies (RoSCTL): Boosts textile exports by reimbursing embedded taxes.
government_policy.regulatory_body: Ministry of Textiles, Government of India; Textile Committee
government_policy.import_export_policy: India generally promotes textile exports through schemes like RoSCTL and PLI. Import policies are designed to balance domestic industry protection with raw material availability, with specific duties on various textile products. India exports cotton yarn and fabrics to countries like Bangladesh, China, Vietnam, and Indonesia.
